refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support validation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report energy metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the enablement of measurement and visibility—what the system must provide—rather than prescribing that the system 'achieve' a target, which depends on operational decisions, equipment tuning, and worksite conditions outside pure system design. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) remains a validation gate, but the FR itself is now decoupled from outcome ownership and focused on the functional capability required.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the system can continuously or periodically capture energy input (e.g., fuel consumed, electrical power drawn) and work output (e.g., tonnage moved, volume excavated, distance traveled under load), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric via operator interface, logs, or telemetry such that a third party can independently verify the calculation against the acceptance criterion over the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: Acquire and log energy consumption data (fuel volume, electrical current/voltage, or equivalent) at defined intervals during operation.
Acquire and log work output data (tonnage moved, volume displaced, distance traveled under load, or equivalent task-specific metric) at defined intervals during operation.
Compute energy efficiency metric (energy_per_ton or equivalent ratio) from logged energy and work output data.
Provide visibility of energy efficiency metric to authorized stakeholders (operator, site manager, tele-operator, maintenance) via user interface, reports, or telemetry.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose energy consumption measurement into (a) instantaneous power acquisition from energy source, (b) continuous integration over evaluation period, and (c) independent work output quantification; correlate via post-processing to derive energy_per_ton ratio.

DP2: Embed energy metering at the energy source boundary (e.g., fuel tank, electrical supply point) and work-output sensing at the point of load application (e.g., tonnage on conveyor, mass lifted); synchronize timestamps and compute ratio in a centralized analytics module.

DP3: Instrument the system with dual independent measurement chains—one for energy (source-level metering) and one for work output (load/displacement sensing)—each with its own data logging and validation; merge logs post-evaluation for ratio calculation and target comparison.

DP4: Implement a real-time energy-to-work ratio monitor that continuously acquires power and work-output signals, computes instantaneous efficiency, logs aggregated statistics, and triggers alerts if running average exceeds E_target; store raw and summary data for post-evaluation audit.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2: Embed energy metering at the energy source boundary and work-output sensing at the point of load application; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio in a centralized analytics module.

potential_sources: Energy metering and work-output sensing are logically independent (different physical phenomena, different sensor types, different measurement principles); decoupling is achievable via timestamp synchronization rather than tight coupling.
Centralized analytics module introduces a single point of dependency for ratio computation, but this is a deliberate architectural choice (not a flaw) that simplifies validation and auditability.
Risk: If energy and work-output sensors share a common power supply or communication bus, failure modes could couple; mitigation: specify independent power and data paths where feasible.
coupling_check.notes: DP2 avoids the real-time constraint of DP4 (which couples measurement to control logic) and the post-hoc correlation complexity of DP1. DP3 is redundant; DP2 subsumes it with cleaner boundaries. DP2 supports independent sensor selection, calibration, and replacement without redesign of the measurement logic.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source boundary is well-defined and accessible for metering (e.g., fuel line, electrical panel). Risk: If energy is distributed (e.g., multiple fuel tanks, hybrid sources), metering complexity increases; mitigation: clarify energy source topology early.

Assumption: Work output is directly measurable at a single point (e.g., tonnage on a scale, displacement via encoder). Risk: If work output is distributed or indirect (e.g., multiple load points, efficiency losses in transmission), measurement may not capture true work; mitigation: validate that point-of-load sensing reflects total useful work.

Assumption: Timestamp synchronization between energy and work-output data streams is achievable with acceptable precision (e.g., ±1 second). Risk: If sensors operate on different clocks or latencies differ significantly, ratio computation may be biased; mitigation: specify clock synchronization method (NTP, GPS, or hardware trigger) and validate drift over evaluation period.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough and operationally representative to smooth transient effects and yield statistically valid energy_per_ton. Risk: Short or atypical evaluation windows may not reflect true efficiency; mitigation: agree on minimum duration and operational profile with customer upfront.

Assumption: Customer-defined E_target is achievable and based on realistic baseline data. Risk: If target is set without engineering validation, system may fail acceptance despite sound design; mitigation: conduct feasibility study and document target rationale.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP2 to explicitly separate concerns: (a) Energy Measurement DP: Acquire instantaneous power at source boundary and integrate over time to yield total energy consumed. (b) Work Output Measurement DP: Quantify tonnage or displacement at point of load application. (c) Data Synchronization DP: Establish common time reference and merge energy and work-output logs. (d) Ratio Computation DP: Calculate energy_per_ton and compare against E_target in a validation module. This four-part decomposition clarifies module boundaries, enables independent testing of each measurement chain, and supports traceability to acceptance criteria. Additionally, specify that energy metering and work-output sensing shall have independent power supplies and data paths to minimize coupling and improve fault tolerance.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy metering, work-output sensing coordination, timestamp synchronization, and ratio computation across the vehicle and teleoperation subsystems

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives energy source measurements (e.g., battery voltage, current) and work-output sensor readings (e.g., motor torque, load force) from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits computed energy efficiency ratios, synchronized timestamps, and analytics results to Vehicle and Teleoperation for operational feedback

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides time synchronization protocol (NTP or equivalent) to ensure consistent timestamp alignment across distributed sensors

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the natural home for centralized analytics, energy metering coordination, and efficiency computation. Sensor data flows in via Telemetry; computed ratios flow out. Minimal diffusion: only timestamp synchronization protocol leaks to Network Link.

Poor containment: Vehicle owns work-output sensing at load application, but energy metering at source (battery/power supply) typically resides outside Vehicle. Centralized analytics would diffuse across Vehicle and external systems. Timestamp synchronization and ratio computation are not Vehicle-specific concerns.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a data aggregation or analytics layer. Energy metering and work-output sensing are distributed across Vehicle and energy source; centralizing analytics in Teleoperation creates artificial coupling and violates separation of concerns.

Poor containment: Safety Services is responsible for hazard detection and mitigation, not energy analytics. While energy efficiency could inform safety thresholds, the core DP (metering, sensing, synchronization, ratio computation) is not a safety-specific function and would diffuse significantly.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the optimal boundary because it is explicitly designed as a centralized analytics and coordination layer. It naturally aggregates heterogeneous sensor inputs (energy source and work-output) via Telemetry, performs timestamp synchronization via Network Link, and computes efficiency ratios without leaking logic into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. This preserves single responsibility and minimizes coupling.
containment_risks: Risk: Sensor data sources (energy metering hardware at source, work-output sensors at load) may be physically or logically distributed across Vehicle and external power infrastructure. NGHE Solution must define clear Telemetry contracts to abstract this distribution.

Risk: Timestamp synchronization across distributed sensors requires a reliable time source (e.g., GPS, NTP). If Network Link is unreliable or latency-prone, synchronization accuracy degrades, affecting ratio computation validity.

Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules cache or pre-process sensor data before sending to NGHE Solution, data quality and synchronization assumptions may be violated. Clear data ownership and flow contracts are essential.

Risk: Real-time analytics performance: if NGHE Solution is remote or cloud-based, latency in Telemetry ingestion and Network Link may introduce unacceptable delays for time-critical efficiency feedback.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP2 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete solution: (1) embed energy metering at source boundary, (2) embed work-output sensing at load application, (3) synchronize timestamps, (4) compute ratio centrally. The DP is logically sound and traces to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Sensor data distribution risk: Energy metering hardware (battery/power supply) and work-output sensors (load application) are physically distributed across Vehicle and external infrastructure. NGHE Solution must enforce strict Telemetry contracts to abstract this distribution; if Vehicle or Teleoperation pre-process or cache sensor data before transmission, data quality and synchronization assumptions are violated. Recommend explicit data ownership and flow specifications in Telemetry interface definitions.

Timestamp synchronization dependency: DP2 relies on Network Link to provide reliable time synchronization (NTP or GPS). If Network Link is unreliable, latency-prone, or unavailable, synchronization accuracy degrades, directly compromising ratio computation validity and acceptance criterion verification. Recommend defining acceptable clock skew tolerances and fallback mechanisms.

Real-time latency risk: If NGHE Solution is remote or cloud-based, Telemetry ingestion and Network Link latency may introduce unacceptable delays for time-critical efficiency feedback loops. Recommend specifying maximum acceptable end-to-end latency for energy_per_ton computation and defining local vs. remote analytics boundaries.

Minimal diffusion to Network Link: Timestamp synchronization protocol (NTP/GPS) leaks to Network Link, but this is acceptable and expected; Network Link is the natural owner of time synchronization infrastructure. Diffusion score of 0.08 for NGHE Solution is justified.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score and is the optimal IM boundary. It naturally aggregates heterogeneous sensor inputs via Telemetry, performs centralized analytics, and outputs computed ratios without scattering DP logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Vehicle (0.45 containment) and Teleoperation (0.4 containment) are poor candidates because they own only partial sensor inputs and lack analytics responsibility. Safety Services (0.35 containment) is inappropriate; energy analytics is not a safety-specific function. Recommended ownership: NGHE Solution.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly verifiable if NGHE Solution correctly implements DP2: (1) energy metering at source provides numerator (energy_used), (2) work-output sensing at load provides denominator (ton_moved), (3) timestamp synchronization ensures paired measurements, (4) centralized ratio computation produces energy_per_ton. Verification requires: (a) calibration and validation of energy and work-output sensors, (b) confirmation of timestamp synchronization accuracy within acceptable skew bounds, (c) test data collection over agreed evaluation period. Feasibility is high if Telemetry contracts and Network Link reliability are established.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns energy metering coordination, work-output sensing coordination, timestamp synchronization, and ratio computation. Vehicle owns work-output sensors at load application and transmits readings via Telemetry. Energy source (battery/power supply) owns energy metering hardware and transmits readings via Telemetry. Teleoperation receives computed ratios via Telemetry for operational feedback. Network Link provides time synchronization infrastructure. However, allocation clarity depends on explicit definition of: (1) Telemetry data contracts (schema, frequency, latency SLAs), (2) Network Link time synchronization protocol and accuracy guarantees, (3) sensor calibration and validation responsibilities, (4) data ownership and pre-processing rules (e.g., Vehicle must not cache or filter sensor data before transmission).

recommended_revisions: Revise DP2 to explicitly specify Telemetry data contracts: define schema, sampling frequency, latency SLAs, and data quality requirements for energy source measurements and work-output sensor readings. Clarify that Vehicle and energy source modules must transmit raw, unfiltered sensor data to NGHE Solution without pre-processing or caching.

Revise DP2 to specify timestamp synchronization requirements: define acceptable clock skew tolerance (e.g., ±10 ms), time source (GPS, NTP, or local atomic clock), and fallback mechanisms if Network Link synchronization fails. Specify how NGHE Solution handles out-of-sync measurements.

Revise DP2 to define real-time latency bounds: specify maximum acceptable end-to-end latency for energy_per_ton computation (e.g., ≤500 ms for operational feedback). If NGHE Solution is remote/cloud-based, define local analytics caching or edge computing strategy to meet latency requirements.

Revise IM boundary documentation to clarify sensor ownership and data flow: explicitly state that energy metering hardware resides at energy source (outside Vehicle), work-output sensors reside at load application (within Vehicle), and NGHE Solution aggregates both via Telemetry. Diagram the physical and logical distribution of sensors and data flows.

Add acceptance criterion for timestamp synchronization accuracy: specify that measured clock skew across all sensors must be ≤ [acceptable tolerance] during evaluation period. This ensures that energy_per_ton computation is based on properly synchronized measurements.

Add acceptance criterion for Telemetry data completeness: specify that NGHE Solution must receive ≥ [threshold]% of expected energy and work-output measurements during evaluation period. This ensures sufficient data for valid energy_per_ton computation.

Revise containment hypothesis to acknowledge Network Link dependency: while NGHE Solution achieves high containment (0.92), its correctness depends on Network Link providing reliable time synchronization. Recommend defining Network Link SLAs (availability, accuracy, latency) as a precondition for DP2 feasibility.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass logically, confirming that DP2 is sound and NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary. However, the gate analysis reveals critical diffusion risks and allocation clarity gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. The recommended revisions are not optional refinements; they are mandatory preconditions for DP2 feasibility and acceptance criterion verifiability. Specifically: (1) Telemetry data contracts are undefined, creating risk of data quality violations and sensor pre-processing leakage; (2) timestamp synchronization requirements lack specificity, leaving clock skew tolerance and fallback mechanisms unspecified; (3) real-time latency bounds are absent, risking operational feedback delays; (4) sensor ownership and physical distribution are not explicitly documented, creating ambiguity in IM boundary allocation. Given high safety criticality, these gaps must be closed at the current level before decomposing further. Proceeding to sub-level FRs without resolving these would propagate ambiguity and diffusion risks into lower-level designs.

revise.why: DP2 must be revised to incorporate explicit specifications for: (1) Telemetry data contracts (schema, sampling frequency, latency SLAs, data quality rules, prohibition on pre-processing/caching); (2) timestamp synchronization requirements (acceptable clock skew tolerance, time source, fallback mechanisms, out-of-sync handling); (3) real-time latency bounds (maximum end-to-end latency for energy_per_ton computation, local vs. remote analytics strategy); (4) sensor ownership and data flow diagram (physical and logical distribution of energy metering, work-output sensing, and aggregation points). These revisions transform DP2 from a logically sound but under-specified solution into an implementable design that explicitly manages diffusion risks and allocation clarity. Revised DP2 will serve as the foundation for sub-level FR decomposition.

assumptions: NGHE Solution has access to reliable Telemetry infrastructure capable of transmitting raw sensor data from distributed sources (energy source, Vehicle load application) without pre-processing or caching.
Network Link provides time synchronization capability (NTP, GPS, or equivalent) with accuracy sufficient to meet clock skew tolerance requirements (to be specified in revised DP2).
Energy metering hardware at energy source boundary and work-output sensors at load application are calibrated and validated before deployment.
Vehicle and energy source modules comply with Telemetry data contracts and transmit unfiltered, timestamped sensor readings without local pre-processing.
NGHE Solution has computational capacity to ingest, synchronize, and compute energy_per_ton ratios within specified real-time latency bounds.
Acceptance criterion verification (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is feasible if Telemetry contracts, timestamp synchronization, and latency bounds are met.
open_risks: Telemetry data quality risk: If Vehicle or energy source pre-process, filter, or cache sensor data before transmission, numerator and denominator measurements become decoupled, invalidating energy_per_ton computation. Mitigation: explicit data ownership rules and Telemetry contract enforcement.

Timestamp synchronization risk: If Network Link synchronization fails or clock skew exceeds tolerance, paired energy and work-output measurements become misaligned, compromising ratio validity. Mitigation: define acceptable skew tolerance, specify fallback mechanisms, add acceptance criterion for synchronization accuracy.

Real-time latency risk: If NGHE Solution is remote/cloud-based, end-to-end latency may exceed operational feedback requirements, degrading efficiency loop responsiveness. Mitigation: specify maximum acceptable latency, define local analytics caching or edge computing strategy.

Sensor calibration and validation risk: If energy metering or work-output sensors are not properly calibrated, measured energy_per_ton will be systematically biased, failing acceptance criterion despite correct DP logic. Mitigation: establish sensor calibration procedures and validation protocols as preconditions for DP2 deployment.

Network Link availability risk: If Network Link is intermittently unavailable, timestamp synchronization and Telemetry data transmission are interrupted, creating gaps in energy_per_ton computation. Mitigation: define Network Link SLAs (availability, accuracy, latency) as preconditions for DP2 feasibility.

Safety criticality amplification: High safety criticality of this FR means that ambiguity in DP2 or IM allocation could propagate into safety-critical sub-systems. Mitigation: resolve all allocation clarity gaps and diffusion risks at current level before decomposing further.
evidence_needed: Revised DP2 with explicit Telemetry data contracts: schema definitions, sampling frequency, latency SLAs, data quality rules, and prohibition on pre-processing/caching.
Timestamp synchronization specification: acceptable clock skew tolerance (e.g., ±10 ms), time source (GPS/NTP/atomic clock), fallback mechanisms, and out-of-sync handling logic.
Real-time latency specification: maximum acceptable end-to-end latency for energy_per_ton computation (e.g., ≤500 ms), and local vs. remote analytics boundary definition.

Sensor ownership and data flow diagram: explicit documentation of physical distribution (energy metering at source, work-output sensing at load application, aggregation at NGHE Solution) and logical data flows.
Network Link SLA definition: availability, accuracy, latency, and time synchronization protocol specifications as preconditions for DP2 feasibility.
Sensor calibration and validation plan: procedures for calibrating energy metering and work-output sensors, validation protocols, and acceptance criteria for sensor accuracy.
Acceptance criteria for timestamp synchronization accuracy: measured clock skew across all sensors must be ≤ [tolerance] during evaluation period.
Acceptance criteria for Telemetry data completeness: NGHE Solution must receive ≥ [threshold]% of expected energy and work-output measurements during evaluation period.
Test plan for DP2 verification: data collection protocol, evaluation period, sensor calibration checkpoints, and energy_per_ton computation validation against E_target.
